title: Dutch West India Company is chartered
blurb: The States General of the Netherlands granted the Dutch West India Company a monopoly over trade and colonization in the Americas and West Africa. The company would govern New Netherland, including the settlement that became New York City.

title: The Schaghen letter records the 'purchase' of Manhattan
blurb: A letter by Dutch official Pieter Schaghen, dated Nov. 5, 1626, reported that colonists had 'purchased' the island of Manhattan from its Native inhabitants for goods worth 60 guilders. It is the only contemporary document of the transaction.

title: Kieft's War erupts with the Pavonia massacre
blurb: On the night of Feb. 25–26, 1643, director-general Willem Kieft ordered Dutch soldiers to attack Lenape refugees camped at Pavonia and Corlears Hook, killing scores of men, women and children. The slaughter ignited a devastating regional war.

title: Treaty ends Kieft's War
blurb: Dutch officials and Lenape leaders concluded a peace at Fort Amsterdam on Aug. 30, 1645, ending more than two years of brutal warfare. The exhausted colony began to rebuild.

title: Deborah Moody founds Gravesend
blurb: Lady Deborah Moody received a patent from director-general Kieft on Dec. 19, 1645, to found the town of Gravesend in what is now Brooklyn. She is regarded as the first woman to hold a land patent in colonial America.

title: Peter Stuyvesant arrives as director-general
blurb: Peter Stuyvesant reached New Amsterdam on May 11, 1647, to take charge of New Netherland. His forceful 17-year rule would reshape the colony until the English conquest.

title: New Amsterdam gains a municipal government
blurb: On Feb. 2, 1653, New Amsterdam was granted a formal municipal charter, establishing a burgher government of a schout, two burgomasters and five schepens. It was the founding of New York City's civic government.

title: The Peach War strikes New Amsterdam
blurb: In September 1655 a large force of Native warriors attacked New Amsterdam and nearby settlements while Stuyvesant was away campaigning against New Sweden. The conflict became known as the Peach War.

title: The Flushing Remonstrance defends religious liberty
blurb: On Dec. 27, 1657, residents of Vlissingen (Flushing, Queens) signed a petition protesting Stuyvesant's ban on Quakers. The Flushing Remonstrance is celebrated as an early American statement of religious freedom.

title: The English seize New Amsterdam, renaming it New York
blurb: On Sept. 8, 1664, Peter Stuyvesant surrendered New Amsterdam to an English fleet under Col. Richard Nicolls without a fight. The city was renamed New York after James, Duke of York.

title: The English seize New Amsterdam and rename it New York
blurb: An English naval squadron under Colonel Richard Nicolls forced Director-General Peter Stuyvesant to surrender New Amsterdam, which was renamed New York for the Duke of York.

title: Jacob Leisler is executed for treason
blurb: Royal authorities convicted Jacob Leisler and his son-in-law Jacob Milborne of treason and executed them by hanging on May 16, 1691.

title: Trinity Church receives its royal charter
blurb: The Anglican parish of Trinity Church was chartered on May 6, 1697, on behalf of King William III by Royal Governor Benjamin Fletcher.

title: Captain Kidd is hanged for piracy
blurb: William Kidd, a onetime respectable New York merchant and privateer, was executed by hanging in London on May 23, 1701, after conviction for piracy and murder.

title: The 1712 slave revolt
blurb: On the night of April 6, 1712, some two dozen enslaved Africans set fire to a building on Maiden Lane and attacked colonists, killing nine.

title: The New-York Gazette, the city's first newspaper
blurb: William Bradford issued the New-York Gazette on November 8, 1725, the first newspaper published in the city.

title: The Montgomerie Charter reorganizes city government
blurb: Governor John Montgomerie sealed the city's third English charter on January 15, 1730, reorganizing New York into seven wards and expanding municipal powers.

title: The Mill Street Synagogue, first in North America
blurb: Congregation Shearith Israel consecrated its Mill Street Synagogue on April 8, 1730, the first purpose-built synagogue in North America.

title: The Zenger trial and freedom of the press
blurb: A New York jury acquitted printer John Peter Zenger of seditious libel on August 4, 1735, a landmark for freedom of the press.

title: Bellevue begins, the oldest US public hospital
blurb: A six-bed infirmary opened on the second floor of New York's new public almshouse, the seed of Bellevue Hospital and the oldest continuously operating public hospital in the United States.

title: The 1741 conspiracy trials
blurb: After a fire at Fort George on March 18, 1741, and more blazes that followed, authorities alleged a plot by enslaved people and poor whites to burn the city.

title: King's College, later Columbia, is founded
blurb: King's College, the future Columbia University, was founded by royal charter of King George II on October 31, 1754.

title: The Stamp Act Congress meets in New York
blurb: Delegates from nine colonies convened the Stamp Act Congress in New York City on October 7, 1765, to protest the Stamp Act.

title: The first Liberty Pole rises in the Common
blurb: New Yorkers raised the first Liberty Pole in City Hall Park on May 21, 1766, to celebrate repeal of the Stamp Act.

title: The Battle of Golden Hill
blurb: British soldiers and Sons of Liberty clashed at Golden Hill on January 19, 1770, an early outbreak of revolutionary violence weeks before the Boston Massacre.

title: The New York tea party
blurb: New York patriots dumped hidden tea from the ship London into the harbor on April 22, 1774, the city's answer to the Boston Tea Party.

title: The Declaration is read to Washington's troops
blurb: The Declaration of Independence was read aloud to Continental Army troops assembled on the Common on July 9, 1776, greeted by three cheers.

title: The statue of King George III is toppled
blurb: After the Declaration was read, a crowd rushed to Bowling Green and pulled down the gilded lead equestrian statue of King George III.

title: The Battle of Long Island
blurb: The Battle of Long Island on August 27, 1776, was the largest battle of the Revolution and a British victory that gave them New York for the rest of the war.

title: The British land at Kip's Bay
blurb: British warships and troops landed at Kip's Bay on Manhattan's East River shore on September 15, 1776, scattering American militia.

title: The Battle of Harlem Heights
blurb: On September 16, 1776, Washington won his first battlefield victory of the war at Harlem Heights in upper Manhattan.

title: The Great Fire of 1776
blurb: A fire burned through the night into September 21, 1776, destroying a large swath of British-occupied New York including Trinity Church.

title: Nathan Hale is hanged as a spy
blurb: The British hanged 21-year-old Continental officer Nathan Hale as a spy in occupied New York on September 22, 1776.

title: The Battle of White Plains
blurb: British and Hessian forces under Howe defeated Washington at White Plains in Westchester on October 28, 1776.

title: The fall of Fort Washington
blurb: British and Hessian forces captured Fort Washington, the last American stronghold on Manhattan, on November 16, 1776.

title: Evacuation Day ends British occupation
blurb: The last British troops left New York on November 25, 1783, and Washington led the Continental Army into the city.

title: Washington's farewell at Fraunces Tavern
blurb: General Washington bade an emotional farewell to his officers in the Long Room of Fraunces Tavern on December 4, 1783.

title: New York becomes the national capital
blurb: The Confederation Congress began meeting in New York, making the city the seat of the young United States government.

title: The African Free School opens
blurb: The Manumission Society opened the African Free School, the first school in New York for the children of enslaved and free Black families.

title: New York ratifies the U.S. Constitution
blurb: Meeting in Poughkeepsie, the state convention voted 30 to 27 to ratify the Constitution, making New York the 11th state.

title: Washington inaugurated at Federal Hall
blurb: George Washington took the oath as the first president on the balcony of Federal Hall in Lower Manhattan.

title: Congress proposes the Bill of Rights
blurb: At Federal Hall in New York, the First Congress approved 12 constitutional amendments and sent them to the states.

title: The Buttonwood Agreement founds the NYSE
blurb: Twenty-four brokers signed a pact on Wall Street to trade securities only among themselves, the origin of the New York Stock Exchange.

title: Aaron Burr charters the Manhattan Company
blurb: The state chartered the Manhattan Company to supply water, but a clause let Aaron Burr use its capital to open a bank.

title: The New-York Historical Society is founded
blurb: Eleven prominent citizens, including Mayor DeWitt Clinton, founded the New-York Historical Society, the city's first museum.

title: Fulton's steamboat sails the Hudson
blurb: Robert Fulton's steamboat, later known as the Clermont, made its first voyage from New York City to Albany.

title: The Commissioners' Plan grids Manhattan
blurb: State commissioners filed the plan that imposed Manhattan's numbered rectilinear grid of streets and avenues above roughly Houston Street.

title: Fulton's steam ferry links Brooklyn
blurb: Robert Fulton's steam ferry Nassau began the first reliable powered crossing between Brooklyn and Manhattan.

title: The city's first savings bank opens
blurb: The Bank for Savings in the City of New-York, the first savings bank in the state, opened to encourage thrift among working people.

title: The Erie Canal opens
blurb: The 363-mile Erie Canal opened, linking the Great Lakes to the Hudson and securing New York's rise as the nation's premier port.

title: Slavery ends in New York State
blurb: An 1817 law took full effect on July 4, 1827, ending legal slavery in New York and freeing thousands of remaining enslaved people.

title: Delmonico's opens
blurb: The Swiss-born Delmonico brothers opened a small café on William Street that grew into America's pioneering fine-dining restaurant.

title: The city's first railroad begins running
blurb: The New York and Harlem Railroad began carrying paying passengers, the first railroad in the city and the world's first street railway.

title: The Great Fire destroys Lower Manhattan
blurb: A fire that broke out on a frigid December night destroyed roughly 674 buildings across 17 blocks of the commercial district.

title: Barnum opens his American Museum
blurb: P.T. Barnum opened his American Museum at Broadway and Ann Street, a wildly popular blend of zoo, spectacle and hoax.

title: New York Philharmonic gives its first concert
blurb: The Philharmonic Society of New York, founded by conductor Ureli Corelli Hill, played its debut concert at the Apollo Rooms on lower Broadway before an audience of about 600. It is the oldest symphony orchestra in the United States.

title: Trinity Church's Gothic Revival spire is consecrated
blurb: Richard Upjohn's third Trinity Church, at the head of Wall Street, was consecrated. Its 281-foot brownstone spire made it the tallest structure in the city, a distinction it held for decades.

title: The Astor Place Riot turns deadly
blurb: A feud between rival Shakespearean actors erupted into a riot at the Astor Opera House that left at least 22 people dead.

title: The New-York Daily Times debuts
blurb: Henry Raymond and George Jones published the first issue of the New-York Daily Times, later The New York Times.

title: Castle Garden becomes an immigrant depot
blurb: The old harbor fort Castle Garden opened as America's first official immigrant processing station.

title: First passenger elevator opens on Broadway
blurb: Elisha Otis installed the world's first successful passenger elevator at the E.V. Haughwout store at 488 Broadway, making it safe to lift people between floors and clearing the way for tall buildings.

title: The Greensward Plan wins Central Park
blurb: Commissioners selected Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux's Greensward Plan as the design for Central Park.

title: City fetes the transatlantic telegraph cable
blurb: New York threw a citywide jubilee for the first transatlantic telegraph cable, with a 100-gun salute, a Trinity Church service and a Broadway parade drawing hundreds of thousands.

title: Lincoln speaks at Cooper Union
blurb: Abraham Lincoln delivered a landmark antislavery address in the Great Hall at Cooper Union that helped propel him to the presidency.

title: The Draft Riots convulse the city
blurb: Resentment over Civil War conscription exploded into four days of rioting that became the deadliest civil disturbance in American history.

title: The Metropolitan Health Act reforms sanitation
blurb: New York created the Metropolitan Board of Health, the first modern municipal health agency in the United States.

title: "The Black Crook" invents the American musical
blurb: A five-and-a-half-hour spectacle fusing melodrama, ballet and song opened at Niblo's Garden and is widely credited as the first American musical, running a record 474 performances.

title: Prospect Park opens in Brooklyn
blurb: The first section of Prospect Park, designed by Olmsted and Vaux, opened to the public in Brooklyn.

title: The first elevated railway opens
blurb: Charles Harvey's cable-powered West Side and Yonkers Patent Railway began running above Greenwich Street, the city's first elevated railroad.

title: The Museum of Natural History is founded
blurb: The American Museum of Natural History was chartered, launching one of the world's great scientific institutions.

title: The Metropolitan Museum of Art is founded
blurb: The Metropolitan Museum of Art was incorporated to build a great art museum and library for the city.

title: The Panic of 1873 shuts the exchange
blurb: The failure of Jay Cooke and Company triggered a panic that forced the first-ever closure of the New York Stock Exchange.

title: Boss Tweed is convicted
blurb: After an earlier hung jury, William "Boss" Tweed was found guilty in his retrial and sentenced to prison.

title: The Natural History Museum opens its home
blurb: The American Museum of Natural History's first purpose-built building opened on the Upper West Side.

title: St. Patrick's Cathedral is dedicated on Fifth Avenue
blurb: Archbishop John McCloskey dedicated James Renwick Jr.'s vast Gothic Revival cathedral at Fifth Avenue and 50th Street. When it opened it was the largest Catholic church in the United States.

title: The Met opens on Fifth Avenue
blurb: The Metropolitan Museum of Art opened its permanent home at the edge of Central Park on Fifth Avenue.

title: Edison lights up Lower Manhattan
blurb: Thomas Edison's Pearl Street Station switched on, the world's first commercial central electric power plant.

title: The Brooklyn Bridge opens
blurb: After 14 years of construction, the Brooklyn Bridge opened as the longest suspension bridge in the world.

title: The Metropolitan Opera opens
blurb: The original Metropolitan Opera House opened at Broadway and 39th Street with a performance of Gounod's Faust.

title: Metropolitan Opera House opens on Broadway
blurb: The original Metropolitan Opera House opened at Broadway and 39th Street with a performance of Gounod's Faust starring soprano Christine Nilsson. It was built by newly wealthy families shut out of the older Academy of Music.

title: The Dakota apartments open on the Upper West Side
blurb: Henry Hardenbergh's Dakota opened at 72nd Street and Central Park West, one of the first luxury apartment houses in the city. Its German Renaissance mass helped make apartment living respectable for the wealthy.

title: The Statue of Liberty is dedicated
blurb: President Grover Cleveland dedicated the Statue of Liberty on Bedloe's Island in New York Harbor.

title: The Great Blizzard of 1888
blurb: A ferocious blizzard buried New York in snow and drifts, paralyzing the city for days and killing roughly 200 people.

title: The New York Botanical Garden is founded
blurb: The state legislature chartered the New York Botanical Garden, modeled on London's Kew Gardens, on 250 acres in the Bronx.

title: Carnegie Hall opens
blurb: Andrew Carnegie's Music Hall opened with a festival featuring Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky as a guest conductor.

title: Ellis Island opens
blurb: The federal immigration station at Ellis Island opened, and Annie Moore of County Cork became the first immigrant processed.

title: The Rapid Transit Act plans a subway
blurb: New York created a Board of Rapid Transit Railroad Commissioners, the legal foundation for the future subway.

title: Voters approve consolidation
blurb: In an advisory referendum, voters across the future five boroughs approved joining together as a single Greater New York.

title: Edison's Vitascope debuts at Koster & Bial's
blurb: The first commercial projection of motion pictures for a paying American audience took place at Koster & Bial's Music Hall on Herald Square, using Edison's Vitascope.

title: The Greater New York charter is signed
blurb: Governor Frank Black signed the charter merging Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, the Bronx and Staten Island into one city.

title: The Brooklyn Museum opens its Beaux-Arts building
blurb: The first section of the Brooklyn Institute of Arts and Sciences building was dedicated on Eastern Parkway, launching what became the Brooklyn Museum. Designed by McKim, Mead & White after a design competition, it was conceived as one of the largest art museums in the world.

title: Five boroughs consolidate into Greater New York
blurb: On New Year's Day 1898 Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, the Bronx and Staten Island merged into a single city of more than three million people.

title: Tenement House Act reforms housing
blurb: New York State's 'New Law' banned the dark, airless dumbbell tenement and required light, ventilation and indoor toilets in new apartment buildings.

title: New York Stock Exchange opens its Broad St. home
blurb: The New York Stock Exchange opened its Classical Revival building at 18 Broad Street, a $4 million temple of finance whose columned facade became a global symbol of American capitalism.

title: Williamsburg Bridge opens over the East River
blurb: The Williamsburg Bridge opened as the longest suspension span in the world, linking the Lower East Side to Brooklyn.

title: General Slocum fire kills more than 1,000
blurb: The steamship General Slocum caught fire in the East River during a church excursion, killing an estimated 1,021 people, mostly women and children.

title: First subway line opens (IRT)
blurb: The Interborough Rapid Transit Company opened New York's first underground subway, running from City Hall to 145th Street.

title: Stieglitz opens 291 and elevates photography as art
blurb: Photographer Alfred Stieglitz opened the Little Galleries of the Photo-Secession at 291 Fifth Avenue, soon known simply as 291. The gallery fought to win art photography the same standing as painting and sculpture in America.

title: Stanford White murdered at Madison Square Garden
blurb: Millionaire Harry Thaw shot architect Stanford White during a rooftop performance at Madison Square Garden, a building White had designed.

title: The Plaza Hotel opens facing Central Park
blurb: Henry Hardenbergh's Plaza Hotel opened at Fifth Avenue and Central Park South. The French Renaissance chateau-style tower quickly became the grandest hotel in the city.

title: First New Year's Eve ball drops in Times Square
blurb: A lighted iron-and-wood ball descended the Times Tower flagpole to ring in 1908, launching an enduring New Year's tradition.

title: Singer Building becomes world's tallest
blurb: The 47-story Singer Building on lower Broadway opened as the tallest building in the world at 612 feet.

title: Hudson-Fulton Celebration fills the harbor
blurb: A two-week civic pageant marked the tercentenary of Henry Hudson's voyage and the centennial of Robert Fulton's steamboat.

title: Uprising of the 20,000 shirtwaist strike
blurb: Thousands of mostly young immigrant women garment workers walked out in the largest strike by women the country had yet seen.

title: The original Pennsylvania Station opens
blurb: McKim, Mead & White's Pennsylvania Station opened to the public, bringing Pennsylvania Railroad trains directly into Manhattan through new Hudson River tunnels. Its waiting room was modeled on the Roman Baths of Caracalla.

title: Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire kills 146
blurb: A fire at the Triangle Waist Company near Washington Square killed 146 garment workers, most of them young immigrant women.

title: New York Public Library's main branch opens
blurb: The Beaux-Arts main library on Fifth Avenue, later the Schwarzman Building, opened with its famous lion-flanked steps and vast reading room.

title: Grand Central Terminal opens
blurb: The Beaux-Arts Grand Central Terminal opened at 42nd Street, replacing an earlier depot with an electrified, double-level rail hub.

title: Armory Show introduces modern art
blurb: The International Exhibition of Modern Art at the 69th Regiment Armory brought European avant-garde art to a shocked American public.

title: Woolworth Building crowns the skyline
blurb: The 792-foot Woolworth Building opened as the tallest building in the world, its Gothic tower dubbed the 'Cathedral of Commerce.'

title: Federal Reserve Bank of New York opens
blurb: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York opened for business in rented Cedar Street offices, becoming the most powerful of the twelve regional Reserve Banks and the Fed's link to Wall Street.

title: New York adopts first US zoning resolution
blurb: New York enacted the nation's first comprehensive citywide zoning law, regulating building height, bulk and use.

title: Black Tom explosion rocks the harbor
blurb: German saboteurs blew up a massive munitions depot on Black Tom Island in New York Harbor, shattering windows across the city.

title: Sanger opens first US birth control clinic
blurb: Margaret Sanger opened the country's first birth control clinic in the Brownsville section of Brooklyn.

title: The Provincetown Players bring O'Neill to the Village
blurb: The experimental Provincetown Players opened their first New York season at 139 MacDougal Street in Greenwich Village, introducing the young playwright Eugene O'Neill to city audiences.

title: Silent Parade marches down Fifth Avenue
blurb: Thousands of Black New Yorkers marched in silence down Fifth Avenue to protest lynchings and the East St. Louis massacre.

title: New York men vote for women's suffrage
blurb: New York became the first major eastern state to grant women the vote when male voters approved a suffrage referendum.

title: Malbone Street wreck kills scores
blurb: A speeding Brighton Beach line train derailed in a tunnel near Malbone Street in Brooklyn, killing at least 93 people.

title: Prohibition ushers in the speakeasy era
blurb: National Prohibition took effect under the 18th Amendment, driving New York's vast drinking culture underground.

title: Wall Street bombing kills 38
blurb: A horse-drawn wagon packed with explosives detonated outside the J.P. Morgan bank on Wall Street, killing 38 and injuring hundreds.

title: Yankee Stadium opens in the Bronx
blurb: Yankee Stadium, 'the House That Ruth Built,' opened in the Bronx and Babe Ruth christened it with a home run.

title: Immigration Act imposes national quotas
blurb: The Johnson-Reed Immigration Act set strict national-origins quotas that sharply curtailed the migration streams feeding New York.

title: First Macy's Thanksgiving parade steps off
blurb: Macy's held its first Thanksgiving morning parade, then billed as the Macy's Christmas Parade, to launch the holiday shopping season at its Herald Square flagship. Employees marched as clowns and cowboys alongside animals borrowed from the Central Park Zoo.

title: The Savoy Ballroom opens in Harlem
blurb: The Savoy Ballroom opened on Lenox Avenue between 140th and 141st Streets and quickly became Harlem's grandest dance hall and the home of the Lindy Hop.

title: Martha Graham gives her first independent concert
blurb: Martha Graham presented her first independent concert at the 48th Street Theatre in Manhattan, performing 18 short works she had choreographed. The evening marked the birth of the company now recognized as the oldest dance company in America.

title: Lindbergh gets a ticker-tape hero's welcome
blurb: Millions lined Broadway for a ticker-tape parade honoring Charles Lindbergh after his solo flight across the Atlantic.

title: Holland Tunnel opens under the Hudson
blurb: The Holland Tunnel opened as the first mechanically ventilated underwater vehicular tunnel in the world, linking Manhattan and Jersey City.

title: "Show Boat" premieres and reinvents the musical
blurb: Jerome Kern and Oscar Hammerstein II's "Show Boat" opened at the Ziegfeld Theatre, weaving serious themes of race and heartbreak into a Broadway musical for the first time. It ran 572 performances.

title: Wall Street crash triggers the Depression
blurb: The stock market collapsed on Black Tuesday, wiping out billions and ushering in the Great Depression.

title: Museum of Modern Art opens
blurb: The Museum of Modern Art opened in a Fifth Avenue office building, dedicated to collecting and showing modern art.

title: Chrysler Building briefly the world's tallest
blurb: The Art Deco Chrysler Building opened at 1,046 feet, its steel spire making it the tallest building in the world.

title: Empire State Building opens
blurb: The 1,250-foot Empire State Building opened as the tallest building in the world, a title it held for four decades.

title: George Washington Bridge spans the Hudson
blurb: The George Washington Bridge opened between Manhattan and New Jersey with the longest main span in the world.

title: Whitney Museum of American Art opens
blurb: Founded in 1930 by sculptor and heiress Gertrude Vanderbilt Whitney, the museum opened on West Eighth Street in Greenwich Village with some 700 works. It was devoted entirely to living American artists.

title: Mayor Jimmy Walker resigns in scandal
blurb: Flamboyant mayor Jimmy Walker resigned amid the Seabury investigation into municipal corruption.

title: The RCA Building opens at Rockefeller Center
blurb: The 70-story RCA Building, the slab-like centerpiece of Rockefeller Center, was completed by Raymond Hood and the Associated Architects. It set the pattern for the Art Deco office slab.

title: Fiorello La Guardia elected mayor
blurb: Reform candidate Fiorello La Guardia won the mayoralty on a Fusion ticket, ending Tammany Hall's grip on City Hall.

title: Apollo Theater opens in Harlem
blurb: The 125th Street Apollo Theater opened catering to Harlem's Black community, debuting with "Jazz a la Carte" headlined by Benny Carter and his orchestra. Its weekly Amateur Night became a launching pad for generations of performers.

title: The Village Vanguard opens
blurb: Max Gordon opened the Village Vanguard in a Greenwich Village basement on Seventh Avenue South, where it grew into the city's longest-running jazz club.

title: Harlem riot exposes Depression grievances
blurb: A false rumor that police had killed a Black teenager sparked a night of unrest in Harlem, leaving three dead and heavy property damage.

title: The Frick Collection opens on Fifth Avenue
blurb: The Fifth Avenue mansion of industrialist Henry Clay Frick opened to the public as an art museum, six years after his widow's death. Architect John Russell Pope had adapted the Beaux-Arts house into a series of galleries around a skylit Garden Court.

title: Triborough Bridge opens
blurb: Robert Moses's Triborough Bridge opened, linking Manhattan, Queens and the Bronx in a vast web of spans and viaducts.

title: Lincoln Tunnel's first tube opens
blurb: The first tube of the Lincoln Tunnel opened beneath the Hudson River, adding a second vehicular crossing to New Jersey.

title: Benny Goodman brings swing to Carnegie Hall
blurb: Benny Goodman's band played a sold-out Carnegie Hall concert, the first time a big band headlined the classical hall and a milestone in jazz's cultural acceptance.

title: The Cloisters opens in Fort Tryon Park
blurb: The Metropolitan Museum of Art opened The Cloisters, a museum of European medieval art perched above the Hudson in Fort Tryon Park. It was built around portions of five French monastic cloisters shipped to Upper Manhattan.

title: New York World's Fair opens in Queens
blurb: The 'World of Tomorrow' fair opened on reclaimed marshland in Flushing Meadows, promising a dazzling technological future.

title: First regular US TV broadcast at the fair
blurb: RCA and NBC televised the World's Fair opening, including President Roosevelt, launching regular US television broadcasting.

title: LaGuardia Airport opens
blurb: North Beach Airport, championed by Mayor La Guardia and soon renamed for him, opened for commercial airline service in Queens.

title: Ballet Theatre gives its inaugural performance
blurb: The company that became American Ballet Theatre gave its first performance at the Center Theatre in Rockefeller Center. Billed as America's first ballet theatre, it aimed to blend the classics with adventurous new work.

title: Queens-Midtown Tunnel opens
blurb: The Queens-Midtown Tunnel opened beneath the East River, linking midtown Manhattan to Queens by car.

title: Normandie burns and capsizes at its pier
blurb: The French liner Normandie, being converted into a troopship, caught fire and capsized at a Hudson River pier.

title: "Oklahoma!" transforms the American musical
blurb: The first collaboration of Richard Rodgers and Oscar Hammerstein II opened at the St. James Theatre, weaving song, story and dance into a single dramatic whole and setting the template for the modern book musical.

title: Harlem riot follows a soldier's shooting
blurb: Unrest erupted in Harlem after a white policeman shot a Black soldier, leaving six dead and widespread property damage.

title: B-25 bomber crashes into Empire State Building
blurb: A B-25 bomber lost in fog crashed into the 79th floor of the Empire State Building, killing 14 people.

title: V-J Day erupts in Times Square
blurb: News of Japan's surrender sent hundreds of thousands into Times Square in a delirious celebration of the war's end.

title: UN chooses New York City for its headquarters
blurb: The United Nations General Assembly voted to site its permanent headquarters in Manhattan after John D. Rockefeller Jr. donated $8.5 million to buy the East River tract.

title: First Tony Awards honor Broadway
blurb: The American Theatre Wing held the first Antoinette Perry Awards in the Grand Ballroom of the Waldorf-Astoria, honoring excellence on Broadway. The awards were nicknamed the "Tonys" during the ceremony itself.

title: Jackie Robinson breaks baseball's color barrier
blurb: Jackie Robinson debuted at first base for the Brooklyn Dodgers, becoming the first Black player in the modern major leagues.

title: Live TV's Golden Age begins with Kraft Theatre
blurb: The premiere of Kraft Television Theatre on May 7, 1947 opened the Golden Age of live television drama, an era of anthology plays broadcast largely from New York studios.

title: Idlewild Airport opens in Queens
blurb: New York International Airport, universally known as Idlewild, opened in Queens and was dedicated by President Truman that summer.

title: Balanchine and Kirstein found New York City Ballet
blurb: New York City Ballet gave its first performance under that name at City Center, growing out of the Ballet Society that George Balanchine and Lincoln Kirstein had started in 1946. The debut program included Concerto Barocco, Orpheus and Symphony in C.

title: "Death of a Salesman" premieres on Broadway
blurb: Arthur Miller's tragedy of the ordinary American, directed by Elia Kazan and starring Lee J. Cobb as Willy Loman, opened at the Morosco Theatre and became a landmark of the modern stage.

title: Birdland opens on Broadway
blurb: Birdland opened on Broadway near 52nd Street with Charlie Parker headlining, taking its name from Parker's nickname "Bird" and becoming a global jazz mecca.

title: The Irascibles rebuke the Metropolitan Museum
blurb: Eighteen avant-garde painters signed an open letter boycotting a Metropolitan Museum of Art exhibition they charged was hostile to advanced art. Published in The New York Times, the protest helped popularize the term Abstract Expressionist.

title: Lever House brings the glass curtain wall to Park Avenue
blurb: Gordon Bunshaft's Lever House, for Skidmore, Owings & Merrill, opened at 390 Park Avenue. Its blue-green glass skin was among the first true curtain walls on an American office tower.

title: "The Honeymooners" premieres on CBS
blurb: Jackie Gleason's sitcom about a Brooklyn bus driver premiered on CBS on October 1, 1955, immortalizing working-class New York in 39 filmed episodes.

title: The Brooklyn Dodgers win their only World Series
blurb: The Dodgers beat the Yankees in Game 7 to claim Brooklyn's first and only World Series championship.

title: Alvin Ailey's company debuts at the 92nd Street Y
blurb: Alvin Ailey and a group of young Black modern dancers gave their first performance at the 92nd Street YM-YWHA, launching what became Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater. The evening included Ailey's early work Blues Suite.

title: The Seagram Building sets the modernist standard
blurb: Ludwig Mies van der Rohe and Philip Johnson completed the bronze-and-glass Seagram Building at 375 Park Avenue. Set back 100 feet behind a granite plaza, it became one of the most influential skyscrapers ever built.

title: "A Raisin in the Sun" opens on Broadway
blurb: Lorraine Hansberry's drama of a working-class Black family became the first Broadway play written by a Black woman when it opened at the Ethel Barrymore Theatre.

title: Frank Lloyd Wright's Guggenheim opens
blurb: The Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum opened on Fifth Avenue, its spiraling white concrete ramp unlike any museum before it. Architect Frank Lloyd Wright had died six months earlier, never seeing it completed.

title: Bob Dylan's first major gig at Folk City
blurb: A 19-year-old Bob Dylan played his first major New York engagement at Gerde's Folk City in Greenwich Village, opening for bluesman John Lee Hooker.

title: The Mets play their first home game
blurb: National League baseball returned to New York City as the expansion Mets opened at the Polo Grounds after the Dodgers and Giants had left in 1957.

title: Saarinen's TWA Flight Center opens at Idlewild
blurb: Eero Saarinen's TWA Flight Center was dedicated at what is now John F. Kennedy International Airport. Its sculptural thin-shell concrete roof, shaped like outstretched wings, made it an icon of the Jet Age.

title: Free Shakespeare opens the Delacorte Theater
blurb: Joseph Papp's Delacorte Theater opened in Central Park with a free production of "The Merchant of Venice" starring George C. Scott as Shylock. Free Shakespeare in the Park has run nearly every summer since.

title: Lincoln Center opens with Philharmonic Hall
blurb: Leonard Bernstein conducted the inaugural concert at Philharmonic Hall, the first building of the new Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts. The event was broadcast live nationally on CBS.

title: First New York Film Festival opens
blurb: The inaugural New York Film Festival opened at Lincoln Center with Luis Buñuel's "The Exterminating Angel," programmed by Richard Roud and Amos Vogel. The ten-day event presented 20 new features to near sellout crowds.

title: Demolition of the original Penn Station begins
blurb: Wreckers started tearing down McKim, Mead & White's monumental Pennsylvania Station, an act that galvanized the American preservation movement.

title: The World's Fair opens in Flushing Meadows
blurb: The 1964-65 New York World's Fair opened in Queens under the theme 'Peace Through Understanding,' drawing more than 50 million visitors over two seasons.

title: The Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge opens
blurb: The world's longest suspension bridge at the time opened, linking Brooklyn and Staten Island across the Narrows.

title: Malcolm X is assassinated in Washington Heights
blurb: The Black nationalist leader was shot dead while addressing his Organization of Afro-American Unity at the Audubon Ballroom in upper Manhattan.

title: The Landmarks Preservation Law is signed
blurb: Mayor Robert Wagner signed the law creating the Landmarks Preservation Commission, spurred by outrage over Penn Station's demolition.

title: Immigration reform reopens the golden door
blurb: President Johnson signed the Hart-Celler Immigration and Nationality Act at the Statue of Liberty, ending national-origin quotas that had favored Europe.

title: The Great Northeast Blackout
blurb: A cascading power failure plunged New York City and much of the Northeast into darkness, stranding 800,000 riders in the subways.

title: The Met moves to Lincoln Center
blurb: The Metropolitan Opera opened its new house at Lincoln Center, leaving the 1883 building on 39th Street for Wallace Harrison's grand modern hall.

title: The Whitney's Breuer building opens on Madison Avenue
blurb: Marcel Breuer's granite-clad Whitney Museum opened at 945 Madison Avenue. Its inverted-ziggurat form and cantilevered upper floors made it a landmark of Brutalist architecture.

title: Joseph Papp's Public Theater opens with "Hair"
blurb: Joseph Papp opened his year-round Public Theater in the former Astor Library on Lafayette Street, launching it with the rock musical "Hair," the first production in its new home.

title: The current Madison Square Garden opens
blurb: The fourth Madison Square Garden opened above the platforms of Pennsylvania Station, built on the site of the demolished Beaux-Arts terminal.

title: Bill Graham's Fillmore East opens
blurb: Promoter Bill Graham opened the Fillmore East in a former Yiddish theater on Second Avenue, making it the premier rock concert hall of its era.

title: Shirley Chisholm is elected to Congress
blurb: The Brooklyn educator became the first Black woman elected to the U.S. Congress, representing Bedford-Stuyvesant.

title: The Ford Foundation opens Manhattan's first atrium garden
blurb: Kevin Roche and John Dinkeloo completed the Ford Foundation headquarters near the United Nations. Its 12-story glass-walled garden was the first great enclosed public atrium in a Manhattan office building.

title: The Stonewall uprising ignites gay liberation
blurb: Patrons fought back against a police raid at the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village, sparking days of protest that launched the modern LGBTQ movement.

title: Arthur Mitchell founds Dance Theatre of Harlem
blurb: Arthur Mitchell, the first Black principal dancer at New York City Ballet, joined with ballet master Karel Shook to found Dance Theatre of Harlem. It became the world's first permanent, professional, predominantly Black ballet company.

title: The Miracle Mets win the World Series
blurb: Just seven years after a record-setting 120-loss debut, the Mets beat the Baltimore Orioles to become the first expansion team to win a World Series.

title: "Sesame Street" premieres from New York
blurb: Created by New York's Children's Television Workshop, Sesame Street debuted on November 10, 1969, set on a fictional inner-city block with an ethnically diverse cast.

title: David Mancuso's Loft ignites disco
blurb: David Mancuso threw a private Valentine's Day party called "Love Saves the Day" at his Broadway loft, an event widely credited with seeding underground dance culture.

title: Knicks win their first NBA championship
blurb: A hobbled Willis Reed limped onto the Madison Square Garden court and the Knicks beat the Los Angeles Lakers in Game 7 for the franchise's first title.

title: The first New York City Marathon is run
blurb: Fred Lebow and Vincent Chiappetta staged the first NYC Marathon as laps around Central Park, drawing 127 starters and just 55 finishers.

title: The World Trade Center's north tower tops out
blurb: Steelworkers set the final beam atop One World Trade Center, briefly making it the tallest building in the world.

title: Ali-Frazier 'Fight of the Century' at the Garden
blurb: Joe Frazier and Muhammad Ali met at Madison Square Garden in the first bout between two undefeated heavyweight champions, won by Frazier.

title: Fania All-Stars ignite salsa at the Cheetah
blurb: The Fania All-Stars played a legendary concert at the Cheetah club, a "Latin Woodstock" that helped define salsa and launch it worldwide.

title: Hip-hop is born in the Bronx
blurb: DJ Kool Herc spun back-to-back breakbeats at a rec-room party at 1520 Sedgwick Ave., an event widely credited as the birth of hip-hop.

title: Nuyorican Poets Cafe is born
blurb: Poet and professor Miguel Algarin opened his Lower East Side apartment to a collective of Puerto Rican writers, founding what became the Nuyorican Poets Cafe. It gave voice to the Nuyorican literary movement and later popularized poetry slams.

title: CBGB opens on the Bowery
blurb: Hilly Kristal opened CBGB at 315 Bowery, its name standing for Country, Bluegrass and Blues. Within two years it became the birthplace of American punk and new wave.

title: "A Chorus Line" opens at the Shubert Theatre
blurb: Michael Bennett's musical about Broadway dancers auditioning for a show, developed at the Public Theater from taped conversations with real performers, moved uptown and became a phenomenon.

title: "Saturday Night Live" debuts on NBC
blurb: NBC aired the first episode of the live sketch show, then titled "NBC's Saturday Night," hosted by George Carlin from Studio 8H at Rockefeller Center. It introduced the "Not Ready for Prime Time Players."

title: 'Ford to City: Drop Dead'
blurb: The Daily News ran its famous headline after President Ford said he would veto a federal bailout of a near-bankrupt New York City.

title: Studio 54 opens
blurb: The Midtown discotheque opened and instantly became the epicenter of celebrity nightlife and disco excess.

title: The blackout that broke the city's spirit
blurb: Lightning strikes knocked out power for some 25 hours, unleashing widespread looting and arson across the crime-plagued, cash-strapped city.

title: The Son of Sam is captured
blurb: Police arrested David Berkowitz, ending a serial-killing spree that terrorized the city for more than a year.

title: Citicorp Center opens, then is secretly reinforced
blurb: Hugh Stubbins and engineer William LeMessurier completed Citicorp Center, a 59-story tower on stilts with a distinctive sloped top. A year later a hidden structural flaw prompted a secret emergency repair.

title: Ed Koch is elected mayor
blurb: The brash congressman won the mayoralty, beginning three terms that spanned the city's recovery from fiscal collapse.

title: John Lennon is murdered outside the Dakota
blurb: The former Beatle was shot and killed by Mark David Chapman at the entrance to his Upper West Side apartment building.

title: The AIDS epidemic surfaces in New York
blurb: The New York Times reported a 'rare cancer seen in 41 homosexuals,' one of the first public alerts to what became the AIDS crisis.

title: MTV launches from New York
blurb: MTV went on the air at 12:01 a.m. on August 1, 1981 from its New York base, opening with the pointed clip "Video Killed the Radio Star."

title: The AT&T Building announces postmodernism
blurb: Philip Johnson and John Burgee completed the pink-granite AT&T Building at 550 Madison Avenue. Its broken-pediment "Chippendale" crown made it the most famous postmodern skyscraper.

title: Bernhard Goetz shoots four on the subway
blurb: A white man shot four young Black men he said were trying to rob him on a downtown IRT train, becoming a lightning rod in the fearful city.

title: The Tompkins Square Park riot
blurb: A clash between police and protesters over a curfew in the East Village park erupted into a night of violence and later findings of police brutality.

title: Museum of the Moving Image opens in Astoria
blurb: The Museum of the Moving Image opened on September 10, 1988 inside a former Paramount building at the Astoria studios, the first US museum devoted solely to film, television and video.

title: The Central Park jogger case
blurb: A young woman was raped and nearly beaten to death in Central Park, and five Black and Latino teenagers were wrongfully convicted of the attack.

title: David Dinkins is elected New York's first Black mayor
blurb: The Manhattan borough president defeated Rudolph Giuliani to become the city's first African American mayor.

title: "Law & Order" premieres on NBC
blurb: Dick Wolf's Law & Order premiered on NBC on September 13, 1990, set and shot in New York City with its signature split between police investigation and prosecution.

title: The Crown Heights unrest
blurb: Three days of violence erupted in the Brooklyn neighborhood after a car in a Hasidic motorcade struck and killed a Black child, Gavin Cato.

title: Terrorists bomb the World Trade Center
blurb: A truck bomb exploded in the parking garage beneath the Twin Towers, killing six people and injuring more than a thousand.

title: "Angels in America" reaches Broadway
blurb: Tony Kushner's epic "Angels in America: Millennium Approaches," set amid the AIDS crisis of 1980s New York, opened at the Walter Kerr Theatre and won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama.

title: Rudolph Giuliani is elected mayor
blurb: The former federal prosecutor narrowly defeated David Dinkins, ushering in a Republican administration focused on crime and quality of life.

title: TWA Flight 800 explodes after takeoff from JFK
blurb: TWA Flight 800 exploded off Long Island shortly after leaving John F. Kennedy International Airport, killing all 230 people aboard. The investigation traced the cause to a center fuel-tank explosion and drove lasting changes to aviation fuel-tank safety rules.

title: The Yankees' dynasty begins
blurb: The New York Yankees won the World Series over the Atlanta Braves, their first title since 1978 and the start of a late-1990s dynasty.

title: Times Square is reborn
blurb: Disney's The Lion King opened at the restored New Amsterdam Theatre, symbolizing the transformation of a once-seedy Times Square into a family tourist mecca.

title: The police killing of Amadou Diallo
blurb: Four plainclothes officers fired 41 shots at an unarmed West African immigrant in the Bronx, killing him and igniting mass protests.

title: 9/11 attacks destroy the World Trade Center
blurb: Al-Qaeda hijackers crashed two jetliners into the twin towers of the World Trade Center, collapsing both and killing 2,753 people in New York City. It was the deadliest terrorist attack in U.S. history.

title: Bloomberg elected mayor after 9/11
blurb: Media entrepreneur Michael Bloomberg won the mayoralty just weeks after the September 11 attacks, defeating Democrat Mark Green. He took office as the 108th mayor on Jan. 1, 2002.

title: Smoke-Free Air Act signed, effective 2003
blurb: Mayor Bloomberg signed a sweeping expansion of the Smoke-Free Air Act, banning smoking in nearly all bars and restaurants. The ban took effect March 30, 2003.

title: Northeast blackout darkens the city
blurb: A cascading grid failure that began in Ohio cut power to New York City and much of the Northeast starting just after 4 p.m. About 50 million people across the U.S. and Canada lost electricity.

title: Staten Island Ferry crash kills 11
blurb: The ferry Andrew J. Barberi slammed at full speed into a concrete pier at the St. George terminal, killing 11 people and injuring dozens. The pilot was found to have been impaired.

title: Freedom Tower cornerstone laid
blurb: A 20-ton granite cornerstone for the Freedom Tower, later One World Trade Center, was laid at Ground Zero. Construction stalled until 2006 amid disputes over money and design.

title: Protests grip Republican convention
blurb: As the Republican National Convention met at Madison Square Garden, an estimated 100,000-plus people marched past the arena in one of the largest protests in U.S. history. More than 1,800 people were arrested over the week, a record for a U.S. political convention.

title: Christo and Jeanne-Claude raise The Gates in Central Park
blurb: The artists Christo and Jeanne-Claude unfurled The Gates, 7,503 saffron-fabric gateways lining 23 miles of Central Park walkways. Conceived in 1979, the project was finally realized for 16 days in February 2005.

title: Transit strike halts subways and buses
blurb: Transport Workers Union Local 100 struck the MTA, shutting down all subway and bus service for three days. Millions of commuters were forced to walk, bike or carpool in December cold.

title: Hearst Tower rises as the city's first green skyscraper
blurb: Norman Foster's Hearst Tower opened above the 1928 Hearst base at Eighth Avenue and 57th Street. It became the first office tower in New York to earn LEED Gold certification.

title: City bans artificial trans fats
blurb: The Board of Health voted unanimously to make New York the first U.S. city to ban artificial trans fats in restaurants. Full compliance was required by mid-2008.

title: Bloomberg launches PlaNYC
blurb: On Earth Day, the city unveiled PlaNYC, a long-range sustainability plan with 127 initiatives to cut emissions, expand parks and prepare for a million more residents. It became a national model for city climate planning.

title: Lehman Brothers collapse jolts Wall Street
blurb: New York-based Lehman Brothers filed the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history, deepening the global financial crisis. The failure of the fourth-largest investment bank rattled markets worldwide.

title: Council extends term limits for Bloomberg
blurb: The City Council voted 29-22 to loosen term limits so Mayor Bloomberg could seek a third term. He signed the measure Nov. 3 and won reelection in 2009.

title: 'Miracle on the Hudson' water landing
blurb: US Airways Flight 1549 lost both engines to a bird strike after takeoff from LaGuardia and ditched in the Hudson River. All 155 people aboard survived.

title: High Line elevated park opens
blurb: The first section of the High Line, a park built on an abandoned elevated freight rail line, opened on Manhattan's West Side. It became a global model for adaptive reuse and spurred a wave of development.

title: Failed Times Square car bomb
blurb: A car bomb left in a Nissan SUV in Times Square failed to detonate after vendors spotted smoke and alerted police. Faisal Shahzad was arrested two days later and sentenced to life in prison.

title: New York legalizes same-sex marriage
blurb: Gov. Andrew Cuomo signed the Marriage Equality Act, making New York the sixth and most populous state to allow same-sex marriage. The law took effect July 24, 2011.

title: Hurricane Irene forces first mass evacuation
blurb: Facing Hurricane Irene, Mayor Bloomberg ordered the city's first mandatory evacuation of low-lying areas and shut the entire subway system. Irene weakened before landfall and spared the city major damage.

title: 9/11 Memorial opens at Ground Zero
blurb: The National September 11 Memorial opened to the public a day after its dedication on the attacks' 10th anniversary. Twin reflecting pools sit in the footprints of the fallen towers.

title: Occupy Wall Street takes Zuccotti Park
blurb: Protesters against economic inequality set up an encampment in Zuccotti Park near Wall Street, popularizing the slogan 'We are the 99%.' Police cleared the camp on Nov. 15.

title: Barclays Center opens in Brooklyn
blurb: The Barclays Center arena opened in Brooklyn as the new home of the NBA's Brooklyn Nets, relocated from New Jersey. Part-owner Jay-Z played the first concerts a week later.

title: Hurricane Sandy devastates the city
blurb: Superstorm Sandy drove a record storm surge into New York City, flooding Lower Manhattan, tunnels and coastal neighborhoods. The storm killed dozens of residents and caused about $19 billion in damage.

title: One World Trade Center reaches full height
blurb: Workers hoisted the final piece of One World Trade Center's spire, bringing the tower to a symbolic 1,776 feet. That November a height panel declared it the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere.

title: Citi Bike launches bike share
blurb: New York launched Citi Bike, then the largest bike-share system in the United States, with 6,000 bikes at more than 300 stations. Riders logged more than 6,000 trips on opening day.

title: Judge rules stop-and-frisk unconstitutional
blurb: A federal judge ruled in Floyd v. City of New York that the NYPD's stop-and-frisk practices violated the Constitution by unlawfully targeting Black and Latino New Yorkers. The court ordered a monitor to oversee reforms.

title: de Blasio elected mayor
blurb: Bill de Blasio won the mayoralty in a landslide, becoming the first Democrat to lead New York City since 1989. He campaigned against a 'tale of two cities' of inequality.

title: 9/11 Memorial Museum opens
blurb: The National September 11 Memorial Museum opened to the public beneath the memorial plaza. It was dedicated May 15 with President Obama and past mayors in attendance.

title: Eric Garner dies in police chokehold
blurb: Eric Garner died after an NYPD officer put him in a banned chokehold during an arrest on Staten Island, gasping 'I can't breathe' repeatedly. A grand jury declined to indict the officer that December.

title: One World Trade Center opens
blurb: One World Trade Center opened for business as publisher Conde Nast moved its first employees into the rebuilt tower. It stood as the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere.

title: Two NYPD officers ambushed in Brooklyn
blurb: Officers Rafael Ramos and Wenjian Liu were shot and killed as they sat in their patrol car in Bedford-Stuyvesant. The gunman, who had cited Eric Garner's death, killed himself in a nearby subway station.

title: "Hamilton" premieres at the Public Theater
blurb: Lin-Manuel Miranda's hip-hop musical about founding father Alexander Hamilton opened Off-Broadway at the Public Theater before a record-breaking transfer to Broadway that August.

title: Pope Francis visits New York City
blurb: Pope Francis made his only visit to New York City, praying at the 9/11 Memorial, addressing crowds and celebrating Mass at Madison Square Garden. Nearly 20,000 people attended the arena service.

title: 432 Park Avenue tops out the Billionaires' Row era
blurb: Rafael Vinoly's 432 Park Avenue was completed as the tallest residential building in the world. Its slender concrete grid of square windows defined the new generation of supertall Midtown towers.

title: Calatrava's Oculus opens at the World Trade Center
blurb: Santiago Calatrava's World Trade Center Transportation Hub, known as the Oculus, opened after years of delay. Its white ribbed dome, meant to evoke a bird in flight, became a striking new civic space downtown.

title: Chelsea bombing injures 29
blurb: A pressure-cooker bomb exploded on West 23rd Street in Chelsea, injuring 29 people. Ahmad Khan Rahimi was arrested days later and sentenced to life in prison.

title: Second Avenue Subway opens
blurb: The first phase of the Second Avenue Subway opened on the Upper East Side, the city's biggest subway expansion in half a century. New stations at 72nd, 86th and 96th streets extended the Q line.

title: Cornell Tech campus opens
blurb: Cornell Tech opened its permanent campus on Roosevelt Island, the product of a Bloomberg-era competition to grow New York's tech economy. The first phase spanned five acres.

title: Truck attack on Hudson River bike path
blurb: A man drove a rented truck down a Lower Manhattan bike path, killing eight people and injuring many others. It was the city's deadliest terrorist attack since 9/11.

title: Amazon scraps HQ2 in Queens
blurb: Amazon canceled plans for a major second headquarters in Long Island City after fierce local opposition. The deal had promised 25,000 jobs and nearly $3 billion in incentives.

title: Hudson Yards opens on the West Side
blurb: The first phase of Hudson Yards, the largest private real estate development in U.S. history, opened over rail yards on Manhattan's West Side. Its centerpiece was the honeycomb-like Vessel structure.

title: Congestion pricing enacted for Manhattan
blurb: New York's state budget authorized congestion pricing for Manhattan south of 60th Street, the first such program in the United States. Revenue was earmarked for the MTA.

title: OMNY brings tap-to-pay to the subway
blurb: The MTA launched OMNY, its contactless tap-to-pay fare system, at 16 subway stations and on Staten Island buses, beginning the long goodbye to the MetroCard.

title: Sweeping rent-law overhaul enacted
blurb: New York enacted the Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act, the strongest tenant protections in state history. It made rent regulation permanent and closed longstanding landlord loopholes.

title: George Floyd protests sweep the city
blurb: Mass protests over the police killing of George Floyd spread across all five boroughs, prompting the city's first curfew since 1943. Marches were mostly peaceful, though some areas saw looting and clashes.

title: Hurricane Ida floods basement apartments
blurb: The remnants of Hurricane Ida dumped record rainfall on New York City in a single hour, overwhelming the sewer system and flooding streets and subways. Thirteen people died, most drowning in basement apartments.

title: Adams elected mayor
blurb: Former police captain and Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams won the mayoralty, becoming the city's second Black mayor. He took office Jan. 1, 2022.

title: Trump convicted in Manhattan hush-money trial
blurb: A Manhattan jury found Donald Trump guilty on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, making him the first former U.S. president convicted of a crime. The verdict came during his 2024 campaign.

title: Mayor Adams indicted on federal charges
blurb: Eric Adams became the first sitting New York City mayor to be federally indicted, charged with bribery and campaign finance offenses. He pleaded not guilty and refused to resign.

title: Congestion pricing tolls begin
blurb: New York became the first U.S. city to charge a congestion toll, with drivers paying to enter Manhattan below 60th Street. The base daytime car toll started at $9.

title: Mamdani wins Democratic mayoral primary
blurb: State Assemblyman Zohran Mamdani scored an upset victory in the Democratic mayoral primary, defeating former Gov. Andrew Cuomo. Ranked-choice results gave him about 56 percent.

title: Mamdani elected mayor
blurb: Zohran Mamdani won the mayoralty at age 34, becoming New York City's first Muslim and first South Asian mayor. He defeated Andrew Cuomo, running as an independent, and Republican Curtis Sliwa.
